title: Why Ancient Egyptian Honey Remains Edible After 3,000 Years
description:
in Film, Food & Drink, History |   	May 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
The globôÙal bee popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion comes up in the news every now and again. Som
body:
in Film, Food & Drink, History |   	May 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
The globôÙal bee popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion comes up in the news every now and again. SomeôÙtimes weãre assured that the numôÙber is staôÙble or risôÙing; more often, weãre warned about colôÙlapsôÙing colonies and the large-scale ecoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal disôÙasôÙter that could result. As with most high-stakes issues, it can be difôÙfiôÙcult to know what to believe. But even if you lack the time to invest in an underôÙstandôÙing of the sciôÙence behind the comôÙplex conôÙnecôÙtions between apiôÙan and human welôÙfare, you can easôÙiôÙly come to appreôÙciôÙate the imporôÙtance of bees if you learn just how long theyãve played a role in our civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion.
As Elana SpiôÙvack writes at History.com, ãa cave paintôÙing in northôÙeastôÙern Spain depictôÙing a human harôÙvestôÙing honôÙey dates back 7,500 years to the NeolithôÙic periôÙod, accordôÙing to research pubôÙlished in 2021 in the jourôÙnal TraôÙbaôÙjos de PreôÙhisôÙtoôÙria.ã Just last year, a paper in the JourôÙnal of the AmerôÙiôÙcan ChemôÙiôÙcal SociôÙetyô conôÙfirmed that bronze conôÙtainôÙers disôÙcovôÙered in an underôÙground shrine in a sixth-cenôÙtuôÙry-BC Greek setôÙtleôÙment not far from PomôÙpeii conôÙtained a residue of honôÙey. Weãve long known of hieroôÙglyphs from ancient Egypt that depict bees and the keepôÙing thereôÙof; ãaccordôÙing to a 2022 paper in the jourôÙnal AniôÙmals, the use of honôÙeyôÙbees in the Nile ValôÙley can be traced to the earôÙliôÙest years of the EgyptôÙian kingôÙdom.ã
Here in the twenôÙty-first cenôÙtuôÙry, most of us regard honôÙey as nothôÙing more than a relôÙaôÙtiveôÙly healthy sweetôÙenôÙer. In ancient Egypt, too, it was used to improve the taste of their bread and beer, but it was also put to imporôÙtant medôÙical uses. ãBecause itãs so thick, rejects any kind of growth and conôÙtains hydroôÙgen perôÙoxôÙide, it creôÙates the perôÙfect barôÙriôÙer against infecôÙtion for wounds,ã writes SmithôÙsonôÙianãs Natasha GeilôÙing. ãThe ancient EgypôÙtians used medôÙiôÙcôÙiôÙnal honôÙey regôÙuôÙlarôÙly, makôÙing ointôÙments to treat skin and eye disôÙeases.ã They may not have been the first to do so, givôÙen that the earôÙliôÙest known uses of honôÙey are recordôÙed on SumerôÙian clay tablets, but they took respect for the stuff to a whole new levôÙel, describôÙing honôÙeyôÙbees as origôÙiôÙnatôÙing from the tears of their sun god Re (forôÙmerôÙly known in the EngôÙlish-speakôÙing world as Ra).
That parôÙticôÙuôÙlar piece of mytholôÙoôÙgy is recordôÙed on some EgyptôÙian papyri; othôÙers reveal how much honôÙey was rationed to workôÙers, at least those employed directôÙly by the Pharaoh. In those days, the subôÙstanceãs goldôÙen colôÙor reflectôÙed its dearôÙness, and it seems that comôÙmon laborôÙers and their famôÙiôÙlies could go a lifeôÙtime withôÙout ever tastôÙing a spoonôÙful themôÙselves. Today, of course, we take it for grantôÙed that we can go down to the superôÙmarôÙket and cheapôÙly buy an econôÙoôÙmy-size tub of honôÙey that nevôÙer goes bad. But then, ancient EgyptôÙian honôÙey has nevôÙer gone bad either: thanks to the very same chemôÙiôÙcal and bioôÙlogôÙiôÙcal propôÙerôÙties that made it useôÙful for healôÙing,ô the sealed jars of it remain theôÙoôÙretôÙiôÙcalôÙly ediôÙble even after 3,000 years. DrizôÙzle it on some genôÙuineô Greek yogurt, and youãve got a large swath of the hisôÙtoôÙry of civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion in breakôÙfast form.
via Boing Boing/SmithôÙsonôÙian
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:

A 3,000-Year-Old Painterãs Palette from Ancient Egypt, with Traces of the OrigôÙiôÙnal ColôÙors Still In It
How SciôÙenôÙtists RecreôÙatôÙed Ancient Egyptãs Long-Lost PigôÙment, ãEgyptôÙian Blueã
Behold 1,600-Year-Old EgyptôÙian Socks Made with NûËlôÙbindôÙning, an Ancient ProôÙto-KnitôÙting TechôÙnique
How Did the EgypôÙtians Make MumôÙmies? An AniôÙmatôÙed IntroôÙducôÙtion to the Ancient Art of MumôÙmiôÙfiôÙcaôÙtion
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: When Francis Bacon Shocked the Art World: Viewers Were Horrified by His Paintings, But Couldnãt Look Away
description:
in Art, History |   	May 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

A difôÙfiôÙcult childôÙhood and adoôÙlesôÙcence, satôÙuôÙratôÙed with the feelôÙing of being an
body:
in Art, History |   	May 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

A difôÙfiôÙcult childôÙhood and adoôÙlesôÙcence, satôÙuôÙratôÙed with the feelôÙing of being an outôÙsider, may or may not conôÙtribute to becomôÙing a great artist. ExpeôÙriôÙencôÙing the social and culôÙturôÙal ferôÙment of Berlin and Paris in the nineôÙteen-twenôÙties probôÙaôÙbly wouldôÙnãt hurt oneãs chances. Nor, sureôÙly, would forôÙmaôÙtive expoôÙsure in such cities to films like MetropôÙoôÙlis, BatôÙtleôÙship Potemkin, and Abel Ganceãsô Napoleon, as well as to the paintôÙings of Pablo PicasôÙso. Going to art school may seem like the natôÙurôÙal choice for any aspirôÙing artist, but thereãs also someôÙthing to be gained from avoidôÙing that acaôÙdôÙeôÙmôÙic sysôÙtem entireôÙly.
These, as galôÙlerist-YoutuôÙber James Payne tells us in the new Great Art Explained video above, are all aspects of the life that proôÙduced FranôÙcis Bacon. As usuôÙal on that series, he proôÙceeds from a sinôÙgle repôÙreôÙsenôÙtaôÙtive work, in this case Study after VelûÀzquezãs PorôÙtrait of Pope InnoôÙcent X, from 1953.
If youãve seen that paintôÙing even once, you havenãt forôÙgotôÙten it, and indeed, youãve probôÙaôÙbly seen it again in your nightôÙmares since. To trace the source of its trouôÙbling powôÙer, Payne plunges into the hisôÙtoôÙry of Baconãs harôÙrowôÙing life as well as that of the Irish, EngôÙlish, and EuroôÙpean hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcal conôÙtexts in which he lived ã often to its danôÙgerôÙous, chaotôÙic fullest.
Not that any art hisôÙtoôÙriôÙan can ignore the inspiôÙraôÙtion citôÙed right there in the paintôÙingãs title. It is to that sevôÙenôÙteenth-cenôÙtuôÙry Spaniardãs acclaimed porôÙtrait of that head of the Catholic Church (who proôÙnounced the finôÙished work ãtropôÙpo veroã) that Bacon pays twistôÙed, deconôÙstrucôÙtive homage. Yet despite havôÙing been to Rome, he nevôÙer actuôÙalôÙly saw the origôÙiôÙnal; that, as Payne explains, ãwould have meant facôÙing its powôÙer directôÙly.ã Instead, he worked from a small, washed-out ãcopy of a copy,ã all the betôÙter to allow for not just reinôÙvenôÙtion, but also the incorôÙpoôÙraôÙtion of othôÙer scraps of the rapidôÙly expandôÙing mass media of the twenôÙtiôÙeth cenôÙtuôÙry: the periôÙod, despite the out-of-time qualôÙiôÙty of so much of his art, to which Bacon so thorôÙoughôÙly belonged.

title: Chuck Jonesã The Dot and the Line Celebrates Geometry & Hard Work: An Oscar-Winning Animation (1965)
description:
in Animation, Education, Film, Math |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The aniôÙmatôÙed short above, The Dot and the Line, directôÙed by the
body:
in Animation, Education, Film, Math |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The aniôÙmatôÙed short above, The Dot and the Line, directôÙed by the great Chuck Jones and narôÙratôÙed by EngôÙlish actor Robert MorôÙley, won an Oscar in 19656 for Best AniôÙmatôÙed Short Film. Based on a book writôÙtenô by NorôÙton Juster,ô ãThe Dot and the Lineã tells the stoôÙry of a romance between two geoôÙmetôÙric shapesãtaking the archeôÙtypôÙal narôÙraôÙtive traôÙjecôÙtoôÙry of boy meets girl, losôÙes girl, wins girl in the end (findôÙing himôÙself along the way) and injectôÙing it with some fasôÙciôÙnatôÙing social comôÙmenôÙtary that still resôÙonates almost fifty years latôÙer. One way of watchôÙing ãThe Dot and the Lineã is as a ãtriôÙumph of the nerdã stoôÙry, where an anxôÙious square (as in ãuncoolã) Line has to comôÙpete with a hipôÙster beatôÙnik SquigôÙgle of a rival for the affecôÙtions of a flighty Dot.
The Line begins the film ãstiff as a stickãÎ dull, conôÙvenôÙtionôÙal and repressedã (as his love interôÙest says of him) in conôÙtrast to the groovy SquigôÙgle and his groovy bebop soundôÙtrack. With the posôÙsiôÙble sugôÙgesôÙtion that this love transôÙgressôÙes mid-cenôÙtuôÙry racial boundôÙaries, the Lineãs friends disôÙapôÙprove and tell him to give it up, since ãthey all look alike anyôÙway.ã But the Line perôÙsists in his folôÙly, indulging in some WalôÙter MitôÙty-like reverôÙies of heroôÙic endeavôÙors that might win over his Dot. FinalôÙly, using ãgreat self-conôÙtrol,ã he manôÙages to bend himôÙself into an angle, then anothôÙer, then a series of simôÙple, then very comôÙplex, shapes, becomôÙing, we might assume, some kind of mathôÙeôÙmatôÙiôÙcal wiz. After refinôÙing his talôÙents alone, he goes off to show them to Dot, who is ãoverôÙwhelmedã and delightôÙed and who ãgigôÙgles like a schoolôÙgirl.ã
Here the subôÙtext of the nerd-gets-the-girl stoôÙryôÙline manôÙiôÙfests a fairôÙly conôÙserôÙvôÙaôÙtive criôÙtique of the ãanarôÙchyã of the SquigôÙgle, whom the Dot comes to see as ãundisôÙciôÙplined, graceôÙless, coarseã and othôÙer unflatôÙterôÙing adjecôÙtives while the lineãwho proôÙclaimed to himôÙself earôÙliôÙer that ãfreeôÙdom is not a license for chaosããis ãdazôÙzling, clever, mysôÙteôÙriôÙous, verôÙsaôÙtile, light, eloôÙquent, proôÙfound, enigôÙmatôÙic, comôÙplex, and comôÙpelling.ã I can almost imagôÙine that George Will had a hand in the writôÙing, which is to say that itãs enorôÙmousôÙly clever, and enorôÙmousôÙly investôÙed in the valôÙues of self-conôÙtrol, hard work, and disôÙciôÙpline, and disôÙtrustôÙful of sponôÙtaneôÙity, free play, and genôÙerôÙal grooviôÙness. At the end of the film, our Dot and Line go off to live ãif not hapôÙpiôÙly ever after, at least reaôÙsonôÙably soã in some cozy subôÙurb, no doubt. The moral of the stoôÙry? ãTo the vecôÙtor belong the spoils.ã
If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!
RelatôÙed ConôÙtentô 
Carl Sagan Explains EvoôÙluôÙtion in an 8ãMinute AniôÙmaôÙtion
Watch ãGeomôÙeôÙtry of CirôÙcles,ã the Abstractô Sesame Streetô AniôÙmaôÙtion Scored by Philip Glass (1979)
JourôÙney to the CenôÙter of a TriôÙanôÙgle: Watch the 1977 DigôÙiôÙtal AniôÙmaôÙtion That DemysôÙtiôÙfies GeomôÙeôÙtry
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: You Can Have Your Ashes Turned Into a Playable Vinyl Record, When Your Day Comes
description:
in Life, Music |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even in death we are only limôÙitôÙed by our imagôÙiôÙnaôÙtion in how we want to go out. The
body:
in Life, Music |   	May 4th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even in death we are only limôÙitôÙed by our imagôÙiôÙnaôÙtion in how we want to go out. There are now ways to turn our corpse into a tree, or have our ashôÙes shot into space, or press our ashôÙes into diaôÙmondsãI believe SuperôÙman is involved in that last one. And now for the music lover, a comôÙpaôÙny called And VinyôÙly will press your ashôÙes into a playable vinyl record.
You like that punôÙny comôÙpaôÙny name? Thereãs more: the busiôÙness lets the dear departôÙed ãLive on from beyond the groove.ã Hear that groan? Thatãs the deceased litôÙerôÙalôÙly spinôÙning in their graveãÎon a turntable.
The UK-based comôÙpaôÙny has been around since 2009, when Jason Leach launched it ãjust for funã at first. But a lot of peoôÙple liked the idea and have kept him in busiôÙness.
It will cost, howôÙevôÙer. The basic serôÙvice genôÙerôÙalôÙly costs between ôÈ1000 and ôÈ3000 GBP, and it partôÙly depends on how many vinyl records you proôÙduce. From what we can tell, you canôÙnot use copyôÙright-proôÙtectôÙed music to fill up the 18ã22 minôÙutes per side. So no ãFree Birdã or ãWe Are the ChamôÙpiôÙons,ã unforôÙtuôÙnateôÙly. But you can put anyôÙthing else: a voice recordôÙing, or the sounds of nature, or comôÙplete silence. Get more inforôÙmaôÙtion over at the comôÙpaôÙnyãs FAQ.
No doubt, the serôÙvice can proôÙvide comôÙfort and a memôÙoôÙry trigôÙger for those left behind. The above video, ãHearôÙing Madge,ã is a short doc about a son who took recordôÙings of his mothôÙer and used And VinyôÙly to make a record out of them. Itãs sweet.
ãIãm sure a lot of peoôÙple think that itãs creepy, a lot of peoôÙple think itãs sacôÙriôÙleôÙgious,ã the man says. ãBut I know my mothôÙer wouldnãt have. She wouldãve thought it was a hoot.ã
Jason Leach, a musiôÙcian and vinyl colôÙlecôÙtor himôÙself, talks of the immeôÙdiôÙaôÙcy of sound and what it means to many.
ãSound is vibratôÙing you, the room, and itãs actuôÙalôÙly movôÙing the air around you,ã he says. ãAnd thatãs whatãs so powôÙerôÙful about hearôÙing someoneãs voice on a record. Theyãre actuôÙalôÙly movôÙing the air and for me thatãs powôÙerôÙful.ã
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2017.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
John Cleeseãs EuloôÙgy for GraôÙham ChapôÙman: ãGood RidôÙdance, the Free-LoadôÙing BasôÙtard, I Hope He Friesã
Bronze Age Britons Turned Bones of Dead RelôÙaôÙtives into MusiôÙcal InstruôÙments & OrnaôÙments
Watch Carl Saganãs ãA GloôÙriôÙous Dawnã Become the First Vinyl Record Played in Space, CourôÙtesy of Jack White
Death: A Free Online PhiôÙlosôÙoôÙphy Course from Yale Helps You GrapôÙple with the Inescapable
 Ted Mills is a freeôÙlance writer on the arts.

title: Gijs Van Vaerenbergh Gracefully Reimagines a 16th-Century Belgian Abbey Church in Steel
description: In the late 12th century, a nobleman named Count Gerard van Loon commissioned an abbey to serve as his final resting place. Over the next few deca
body:
In the late 12th century, a nobleman named Count Gerard van Loon commissioned an abbey to serve as his final resting place. Over the next few decades, amid plenty of political tumult, Herkenrode Abbey in Hasselt, Belgium, was converted to the first Cistercian convent for women. It was a site of pilgrimage from the 13th to the 15th centuries, and despite regional wars and economic uncertainty, it stayed the course. During the 16th century, it experienced its heyday thanks to the patronage of a figure named Prince Bishop Evrard van der Marck, seeing the addition of a Gothic church that brimmed with beautiful stained glass windows, textiles, paintings, and more.
The Eighty Yearsã War paused Herkenrodeãs prosperity, and once things stabilized again politically, the abbey experienced several decades of good fortune, although much of this wealth was spent on the abbessesã own acquisitions of property and art in a show of their prestige. But the paradigm-shifting Liû´ge Revolution, which coincided with the French Revolution, brought all of this crashing to a halt by 1796. The abbey complex was promptly sold and dismantled.

Throughout the 19th and 20th centuries, Heckenrode went through many other uses, including factories and even a series of private homes, before it was again acquired by a religious organization in the 1970s. While the original 16th-century abbey church no longer exists due to a devastating fire in 1826, the site remains one of the regionãs most culturally significant. And Herita has been working to restore it. As part of a phased regeneration of the landmark and its park, an ethereal, life-size sculpture of the abbey titled CLAUSURA by Gijs Van Vaerenbergh has risen from the buildingãs original footprint.
The studio, founded by Belgian designers Pieterjan Gijs and Arnout Van Vaerenbergh, conceived of CLAUSURA as ãan artistic vision for the vanished heart of Herkenrode.ã True to scale, the structure is made of slender steel rods that rise from the ground in an airy framework. The installation revolves around the idea of memory and sensation: rather than rebuilding the abbey to try to mirror what it may have looked like hundreds of years ago, the work nods to its past with an airy elegance. 
ãThe new volumes are transparent, allowing their silhouettes to blend seamlessly with the landscape in the background,ã says a statement. ãThe intervention balances between reconstruction and abstraction, as the original structures are evoked through a refined play of suggestion. Iconic details such as windows, vaults, and towers enhance the sense of recognisability, although at times, these elements dissolve back into a chaos of lines.ã

Gijs Van Vaerenbergh is known for its architectural interventions, often utilizing steel, stone, wood, and a wide range of other building materials to re-envision spaces as structural sculptures. ãWhat unites their diverse output is a sustained focus onô how space is experiencedãvisually, bodily and temporally,ã a statement says.
CLAUSURA is being constructed in three phases. The first, which is also the most ambitious, is slated to open to the public on June 18. Visitors will be able to walk and reflect amid the installation. See more on Gijs Van Vaerenberghãs Instagram, and learn about the restoration progress and how to visit on Heritaãs website.

schema.headline: Gijs Van Vaerenbergh Gracefully Reimagines a 16th-Century Belgian Abbey Church in Steel

title: Wander through Adrienna Matzeg's Embroidered, Late-Night City Explorations
description: When Adrienna Matzeg embarked on a trip to Kyoto, Tokyo, and Seoul in July 2025, she encountered intense midsummer heat and humidity, which led he
body:
When Adrienna Matzeg embarked on a trip to Kyoto, Tokyo, and Seoul in July 2025, she encountered intense midsummer heat and humidity, which led her to exploring some of the citiesã nooks and crannies in the dark, when it was cooler. 
Illuminated storefronts and signage characterize the artistãs late-night runs to convenience stores, markets, and other features of these hubsã sprawling  urban fabric. ãIn her textile embroidery work, however, the energy of the city falls away,ã says a statement from Abbozzo Gallery, which presents her forthcoming solo exhibition, After Hours. ãWhat remains are quiet scenes that left an imprint, tactile snapshots as a record of those summer nights.ã
ãLate Night Snackã
After Hours represents an evolution of Matzegãs travel-related works, centered around embroidered vignettes of snacks and roadside attractions that have a snapshot-like, diaristic quality. Brightly lit facades and bold displays seem to float on the surface of black linen, with the addition of a small plate of hanami dangoãthe distinctive pink, white, and green rice dumpling snack on a stickãdrawing us close to the artistãs experience.
After Hours runs from May 8 to 30 in Toronto. See more on the artistãs Instagram.

title: Vibrant Victorian-Era Transparencies Illuminate a Host of Microscopic Creatures
description: During the Victorian era, innovators made huge leaps with optical technologies. It was the period of the stereoscope and an early projector known
body:
During the Victorian era, innovators made huge leaps with optical technologies. It was the period of the stereoscope and an early projector known as the magic lantern, not to mention one in which eyeglasses became more affordable and entering the mainstream. These advances also influenced scientific inquiry, making microscopes more powerful, and the pursuit of microscopy enabled researchers and enthusiasts to discover creatures invisible to the naked eye.
One of these enthusiasts was London-based educator and amateur scientist Charles Thomas Hudson. Along with other scholars and aficionados, he participated in interest groups. ãAs President of the Royal Microscopical Society and a Fellow of the Royal Society, Hudson was a leading figure in this growing scientific community,ã says a statement from Osh Gallery, which is currently exhibiting a collection of unique illustrations in The Hudson Transparencies.
Sea slugs
Curated byô Luke Powell and Jody Hudson-Powell of design firm Pentagram, which runs Osh Gallery, the exhibition literally brings to light a number of colorful transparencies that Hudson used during his lectures. While they appear dark and even unfinished in a typical setting, ãwhen lit from behind these intricate works transform into magical visions of life previously only glimpsed when viewed under a microscope,ã the gallery says.
The Hudson Transparencies includes 58 original transparencies that measure a surprisingly large 37.8 by 29.5 inches. Each of the graphicsã proportions are ãthe equivalent of drawing ants the size of elephants,ã says a statement. The animals and botanicals emerge through a combination of painted paper and perforations, which are made with lines and clusters of pinholes.
Hudson was particularly fascinated by rotifers, a phylum of zooplankton named for their so-called ãwheel-bearingã characteristics. He also catalogued algae, protozoa, and larvaeãsuch as that of the mayfly with its feather-like tail. Microscopic marine organisms were a particular favorite, though, and these back-lit images highlight the convergence of science and spectacular visuals that not only brought these creatures to life in a unique way for 19th-century viewers but continue to awe us today.
The Hudson Transparencies continues through June 11 in London. You might also enjoy Martin Kunzãs turn-of-the-century tactile graphics and Lorenz Okenãs seminal natural history work,ô Allgemaine Naturgeschichte Fû¥r Alle StûÊnde.

A larva of a mayfly
A collection of rotifer species
Testudinella patina
Sponges, Porifera
Proales werneckii
Cupelopagis vorax
